1.synthesis:
Having specified the sacred furniture, God turns to the structure that would house it. The tabernacle's basic form was a layered tent: an innermost covering of fine linen and wool, then a second layer of goat-hair fabric, then outer coverings of ram skins dyed red and sea-cow hides. These four layers created a dwelling at once beautiful within and weather-resistant without — a portrait of glory veiled.

The innermost curtains — ten in number, each twenty-eight cubits long and four cubits wide — were woven of fine twisted linen in blue, purple, and scarlet, with images of cherubim skillfully worked throughout (Exod 26:1). The five curtains were coupled together with fifty golden clasps into one great panel, and then the two panels were joined at the center by fifty clasps of gold (Exod 26:6). This inner covering was what worshippers inside the holy place would see overhead — a canopy of cherubim in richest colors, suggesting the heavenly courts.

Over this lay eleven curtains of goat hair, each thirty cubits by four (Exod 26:7-8), joined likewise with fifty clasps, but these of bronze (Exod 26:11). The goat-hair fabric, darker and coarser, formed a tent over the tabernacle, with a half-curtain folded back over the entrance. Two outer coverings completed the structure: tanned ram skins and sea-cow hides (Exod 26:14), wholly utilitarian, giving no external indication of the splendor within.

Gill sees the multilayered structure as a type of the incarnation: "this tabernacle was a type both of the human nature of Christ, which is the true tabernacle which God pitched, and not man." The contrast of outward plainness and inward glory is precisely the paradox of the incarnate Word — "he had no form or majesty that we should look at him" (Isa 53:2) from without, yet within, "the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily" (Col 2:9).
1.historical_context:
Tent-shrines were known throughout the ancient Near East; the portable tent (the Ohel mo'ed, tent of meeting) fit Israel's nomadic life in the wilderness. Egyptian parallels include Ramesses II's portable tent-sanctuary carried on campaigns, suggesting such structures were recognizable cultural forms. The rich inner coverings reflect the same aesthetic as Egyptian temple interiors — walls painted with divine beings — while the outer plainness suited a desert environment. The goat-hair covering is practically identical to the black tents still used by Bedouin; it shed rain while allowing airflow. The ram-skin and sea-cow layers provided waterproofing and durability for the multi-decade journey.

The fifty golden clasps joining the inner panels recall the numerological significance of fifty in Levitical tradition (jubilee, Pentecost), though here the function is structural unity: two separate sets of curtains become one dwelling-place.
1.literary_structure: The curtain passage subdivides neatly: the inner linen curtains (vv 1-6), the goat-hair tent curtains (vv 7-13), and the outer skins (v 14). Each section specifies number, dimensions, connectors, and arrangement. The gold clasps of the inner curtains give way to bronze clasps for the outer — a gradient from most to least precious that mirrors the holiness gradient from ark to courtyard. The structural climax is the joining of the two great panels into one unified tabernacle (v 6), an act of intentional assembly that makes a dwelling from separate components.
1.theological_application:
Matthew Henry draws the ecclesiological implication: the tabernacle is "a type also of the church of God, which is his house and dwelling-place." The beauty visible only from within — the cherubim woven into the innermost curtain, unseen from outside — corresponds to the church's hidden glory, known only to those who have entered. Peter writes that believers are "a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession" (1 Pet 2:9) — dignity invisible to the world but real before God.

The fifty golden clasps unifying the curtains speak to the unity of the church. Separate panels become one dwelling only when properly joined; so the body of Christ is one only when each member is connected to the others in the unity the Spirit gives (Eph 4:3). The goat-hair covering above the beauty may also speak to the covering of sin by sacrifice — the goat being the primary animal of sin-offering in the Levitical system.
1.christology: The contrast between the tabernacle's external plainness and its internal splendor is a parable of the incarnation. Gill is explicit: the tabernacle is "a type both of the human nature of Christ, which is the true tabernacle which God pitched, and not man." The letter to the Hebrews identifies Christ as the one who has pitched the "greater and more perfect tent" (Heb 9:11). Just as the tabernacle's inner curtains bore the images of cherubim — the heavenly court pressed into cloth — so in Christ "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Col 2:3), invisible to those who see only his outward form. The richly colored linen within points to what John saw at the transfiguration: "his face shone like the sun, and his clothes became white as light" (Matt 17:2), the glory ordinarily concealed beneath human flesh briefly unveiled.

15.synthesis:
The curtains alone could not stand; they required a rigid framework. God now specifies forty-eight upright boards of acacia wood, each ten cubits tall and one and a half cubits wide, overlaid with gold and set in silver bases (Exod 26:15-19). Twenty frames ran along the south side, twenty along the north, and six across the west, with two corner frames to square the building — each board held in its silver socket by two tenons. Across the boards ran five horizontal bars of acacia overlaid with gold, the middle bar running the full length of each wall from end to end (Exod 26:26-29).

Keil and Delitzsch note that the boards were made "so that they could stand upright," each anchored in a pair of silver bases — 96 bases in all for the three walls. The silver came from the half-shekel atonement ransom paid by every Israelite male (Exod 30:11-16; Exod 38:25-28), so that the very foundation of the tabernacle rested on ransom money. The dwelling of God was literally supported by the price of redemption.

The five horizontal bars held the upright boards together in structural solidarity. The middle bar, threading through the center of each board "from end to end" (Exod 26:28), was the great unifying member. Gill sees in the boards "the firmness and security" of the house: without the framework "the curtains would have been blown about by every wind." The church requires structure as well as covering; beauty without support collapses.
15.historical_context: The exact construction has been debated among scholars: some read the qerāšim (boards/frames) as solid planks, others as open frames or lattice. Keil and Delitzsch favor planks thick enough to stand, noting the combined weight of the gold overlay. If the boards were one cubit wide (roughly 18 inches) and the tabernacle had twenty per long side, the exterior walls would be thirty cubits long — matching the curtain lengths. The silver bases, two per board, weighed one talent each for the entire set (Exod 38:27); this was the exact amount of silver collected from the whole census of Israel's fighting men, one talent = 3,000 shekels = 600,000 half-shekels from 603,550 men (Exod 38:25-26). The symbolic coincidence is too precise to be accidental: the entire adult male population of Israel provided the foundation of the tabernacle through their ransom payments.
15.literary_structure: The frame passage subdivides by wall: south (vv 18-19), north (vv 20-21), west (vv 22-25), and then the system of horizontal bars (vv 26-29), concluding with the summary command to follow the pattern shown on the mountain (v 30). The repetition of "twenty boards" for south and north, then "six boards" for west plus two corner boards, gives the section a precise architectural rhythm. The bars are presented last, as the integrating element — what holds the individual frames together into a unified whole.
15.theological_application:
Matthew Henry notes that the boards were overlaid with gold while standing in silver sockets: they were precious within their function, not mere scaffolding. The church's "structures" — eldership, order, doctrine — are likewise to be precious and weighty, not merely utilitarian. The silver-socket foundation drawn from ransom money is a permanent reminder that the community of God's people stands on redemption, not on natural merit or human achievement.

The middle horizontal bar threading the full length of each wall speaks to what Paul calls the "bond of perfectness" — love and truth that run through every part of the community and hold it together (Col 3:14). No board stood alone; each was joined to its neighbors by shared bars and rested in the same silver foundation.
15.christology:
Gill identifies the boards as figures of "believers in Christ, who are compared to trees of righteousness, the planting of the Lord." Just as the acacia boards were cut from living trees, seasoned, overlaid with gold, and set in silver — so believers are taken from natural life, transformed by grace, clothed in Christ's righteousness, and grounded in the redemption he purchased. The acacia wood itself, imperishable in desert conditions, is an ancient symbol of incorruptibility; it points to the resurrection body of Christ and of his people — imperishable, raised in glory (1 Cor 15:42-43).

The silver sockets — ransom money — underlie every board of the tabernacle without exception, visible only at floor level, unseen by those who look only at the golden surface. So the atonement of Christ is the unseen but load-bearing foundation of everything in the life of the church; every spiritual gift, every ministry, every member rests on what was paid at the cross.

31.synthesis:
Between the holy place and the most holy place hung the great veil: blue, purple, and crimson yarn on fine twisted linen, with cherubim woven into its surface by a skilled craftsman (Exod 26:31). It hung on four gold-overlaid acacia posts in silver sockets (Exod 26:32), and behind it — in the most holy place — were placed the ark and mercy seat (Exod 26:33-34). In front of it, on the other side, stood the table on the north and the lampstand on the south (Exod 26:35). A second curtain — simpler, on five posts — served as the entrance screen to the holy place from the courtyard (Exod 26:36-37).

The veil is the most theologically charged piece of textile in all of Scripture. Keil and Delitzsch describe its purpose: to "divide the holy place from the most holy place in the tabernacle," marking the boundary between the presence God shared with the priests and the presence God reserved for the high priest alone on the Day of Atonement. The cherubim woven into it recalled the cherubim guarding Eden's entrance after the fall (Gen 3:24) — a visual declaration that the way to God's immediate presence was barred to sinful humanity.

Gill catalogues the veil's typological meanings with characteristic care. First, it represents sin: "the sin of man, which separates between God and man, was removed by the death of Christ when the vail was rent, and so the way to heaven opened." Second, it represents the obscurity of the old covenant: "the obscurity of the legal dispensation, the Gospel being veiled under the shadows of the law." Third — and most powerfully — it represents Christ's human nature: "the human nature of Christ, his flesh, called in allusion to it the vail of his flesh" (Heb 10:20).

This third identification is the most astonishing: the very thing that separates becomes, in Christ, the way of entry. Through his flesh — through the incarnation and the tearing of that flesh in death — access to the holiest is opened. The veil both divided and, when rent, revealed. Matthew Henry writes: "The rending of the vail, when Christ died, signified both that Christ was the true temple, the true tabernacle, which, being now revealed, put an end to the typical one."
31.historical_context:
The inner veil was so thick and substantial that the Mishnah describes it as requiring three hundred priests to immerse it at ritual purification (Shekalim 8:5 — a likely exaggeration emphasizing its weight). Roman-era sources (Josephus, War 5.212) describe the Jerusalem temple veil as sixty feet tall and four inches thick. The Mosaic tabernacle's version was proportionally smaller but equally dense, woven six threads thick according to rabbinic tradition. Its function was absolute visual separation: no one could see into the most holy place while standing in the holy place.

The entrance curtain (vv 36-37) used the same colors but a different technique — "embroidered" (NAS) rather than the "artistic work" of the inner veil. Its five posts in bronze sockets (not silver) placed it in a lower holiness register than the inner veil's four posts in silver. The gradient of five posts → four posts, bronze → silver, points inward toward increasing sacred intensity.
31.literary_structure: The passage moves in two steps: the inner veil and its placement (vv 31-35) and the outer entrance screen (vv 36-37). The inner veil passage is the climax of the entire chapter, as it is the object that most directly defines the spatial and theological structure of the whole tabernacle. The arrangement of furniture relative to the veil (v 35: table north, lampstand south) integrates the furnishings of ch 25 with the structure of ch 26 into a single coherent spatial order.
31.theological_application:
Gill's threefold typology of the veil is the richest available: it signifies sin-separation (removed by the cross), legal obscurity (removed by the gospel's clarity), and Christ's flesh (the very means of entry). The author of Hebrews holds all three together in Hebrews 10:19-20: "we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain, that is, through his flesh." The veil torn at the crucifixion (Matt 27:51) is not the destruction of a barrier but the fulfillment of what it pointed to — the opened way is through Christ's body, not around it.

The church now lives on the far side of that torn veil, with "boldness to enter the most holy place" that was impossible for the ordinary Israelite. The cherubim woven into the veil no longer bar the way; Christ has passed through death and resurrection and opened access for all who come through him.
31.christology:
The veil is perhaps the single richest Christological symbol in the Mosaic sanctuary. In his death, the temple veil was "torn in two, from top to bottom" (Matt 27:51) — torn from above, by divine act, not from below by human effort. Gill writes: "the vail of his flesh, called in allusion to it the vail of his flesh, Hebrews 10:20." The incarnation is the veil: God dwelling in human flesh as the mediating boundary between divine holiness and human approach. The crucifixion is the rending: that flesh torn opens the way that cherubim had guarded since Eden.

Hebrews 9 presses the contrast: the high priest entered the most holy place once a year, alone, with blood not his own (Heb 9:7); Christ entered "once for all into the holy places... by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption" (Heb 9:12). The tabernacle's most holy place was the place only God could inhabit and only the high priest could enter once a year; in Christ, it becomes the place every believer can approach with confidence every day.
